turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Strategic group
– A set of business units or firms that pursue similar strategies with similar resources
Strategic Types
• Defenders
Are companies with a limited product line that focus on improving the efficiency of their existing operations. This cost orientation makes them unlikely to innovate in new areas. With its emphasis on efficiency, LINCOLN electric is an example of a defender
• Prospectors
Are companies with fairly broad product lines that focus on product innovation and market opportunities. This sales orientation makes them somewhat inefficient. They tend to emphasize creativity over efficiency. Frito Lay’s emphasis on new product development makes it an example of a prospector.
• Analyzers
Are corporations that operate in at least two different product-market areas, one stable and one variable. In the stable areas, efficiency is emphasized. In the variable areas, innovation is emphasized. Multidivisional firms, such as BASF and Procter & Gamble, which operate in multiple industries, tend to be analyzers.
• Reactors
Lack a consistent strategy-structure-culture relationship
EX: jet Blue Airlines

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text: Yes, Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) can be formulated based on the information provided in this part.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Here are some possible MCQs based on the information provided in this part:

1. What is a strategic group?
a. A set of business units or firms with different strategies and resources
b. A set of business units or firms with similar strategies and resources
c. A set of business units or firms with different resources but similar strategies
d. A set of business units or firms with similar resources but different strategies

Answer: b. A set of business units or firms with similar strategies and resources

2. Which of the following strategic types focuses on improving operational efficiency and has a limited product line?
a. Defenders
b. Prospectors
c. Analyzers
d. Reactors

Answer: a. Defenders

3. Which of the following strategic types focuses on product innovation and has a broad product line?
a. Defenders
b. Prospectors
c. Analyzers
d. Reactors

Answer: b. Prospectors

4. Which of the following strategic types operates in at least two different product-market areas, one stable and one variable, and emphasizes both efficiency and innovation?
a. Defenders
b. Prospectors
c. Analyzers
d. Reactors

Answer: c. Analyzers

5. Which of the following strategic types lacks a consistent strategy-structure-culture relationship?
a. Defenders
b. Prospectors
c. Analyzers
d. Reactors

Answer: d. Reactors

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Porter’s Five forces framework is a method for analyzing competition of a business. It determines five forces that describe the competitive intensity and therefore the attractiveness of an industry in terms of its profitability.
• Threat of new entrants
– New entrants to an industry bring new capacity, a desire to gain market share and substantial
resources
Entry barrier
– An obstruction that makes it difficult for a company to enter an industry Barriers to Entry
Some of the possible barriers to entry are:
– Economies of scale
Scale economies in the production and sale of microprocessors, for example, gave Intel a significant cost
advantage over any new rival.
– Product differentiation
Corporations such as Procter & Gamble and General Mills, which manufacture products such as Tide and Cheerios, create high entry barriers through their high levels of advertising and promotion.
               •
– Capital requirements
The need to invest huge financial resources in manufacturing facilities in order to produce large commercial
airplanes creates a significant barrier to entry to any competitor for Boeing and Airbus.
– Switching costs
Once a software program such as Excel or Word becomes established in an office, office managers are very
reluctant to switch to a new program because of the high training costs.
– Access to distribution channels
Small entrepreneurs often have difficulty obtaining supermarket shelf space for their goods because large retailers charge for space on their shelves and give priority to the established firms who can pay for the advertising needed to generate high customer demand.
– Cost disadvantages independent of size
Once a new product earns sufficient market share to be accepted as the standard for that type of product, the maker has a key advantage. Microsoft’s development of the first widely adopted operating system (MSDOS) for the IBM- type personal computer gave it a significant competitive advantage over potential competitors. Its introduction of Windows helped to reinforce that advantage so that the Microsoft operating system is now on more than 90% of personal computers worldwide
– Government policies
Governments can limit entry into an industry through licensing requirements.
Rivalry Among Existing Firms
– In most industries, corporations are mutually dependent.
– A competitive move by one firm can be expected to have a noticeable effect on its competitors and thus may cause retaliation.
      
               •
Threat of Substitute Products or Services
Substitute product
– A product that appears to be different but can satisfy the same need as another product
The identification of possible substitute products means searching for products that can perform the same
function, even though they have a different appearance.
The Bargaining Power of Buyers
– Buyers affect an industry through their ability to force down prices, bargain for higher quality or more services, and play competitors against each other.
•
•
Bargaining power of buyers:
• Large purchases: A buyer purchases a large proportion of the seller’s product or service (ex: oil filters purchased by a major automaker)
• Backward integration: A buyer has the potential to integrate backward by producing the product itself (ex: a newspaper could produce its own paper)
• Alternative suppliers: Alternative suppliers are plenty because the product is standard or undifferentiated (ex: motorists can choose among many gas stations)
• Low cost to change suppliers: Changing suppliers costs very little (ex: office supplies are easy to find)
• Product represents a high percentage of buyer’s cost: The purchased product represents a high percentage of
a buyer’s costs, thus it is worth looking for a lower price.
• Buyer earns low profits: “Cost/service sensitive” A buyer earns low profits and is thus very sensitive to costs & service differences (grocery stores have very small gross margins- company's net sales revenue minus its cost of goods sold)
• Product is unimportant to buyer: The purchased product is unimportant to the final product quality, thus can be easily substituted (electric wire for use in lamps)
     
• The Bargaining Power of Suppliers
– Suppliers can affect an industry through their ability to raise prices or reduce the quality of
purchased goods and services.
A buyer or a group of buyers is powerful if some of the following factors hold true:
– Industry is dominated by a few companies
– Unique product or service
– Substitutes are not readily available
– Ability to forward integrate
– Unimportance of product or service to the industry

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Yes, MCQs can be formulated based on the information provided in this part. Here are some possible MCQs:

1. What is the purpose of Porter's Five Forces Framework?
a. To analyze competition within an industry
b. To determine consumer preferences and needs
c. To evaluate a company's financial performance
d. To assess the impact of technology on a market

Answer: a. To analyze competition within an industry

2. Which of the following is NOT a barrier to entry?
a. Economies of scale
b. Product differentiation
c. High demand from customers
d. Switching costs

Answer: c. High demand from customers

3. Which of the following is a substitute product?
a. Tide detergent
b. Cheerios cereal
c. Paper towels
d. Cloth napkins

Answer: d. Cloth napkins (as they can perform the same function as paper towels)

4. What is the bargaining power of buyers?
a. The ability of buyers to force down prices or bargain for higher quality/services
b. The ability of suppliers to raise prices or reduce quality
c. The mutual dependence of corporations within an industry
d. The threat of new entrants bringing new capacity and resources 

Answer: a. The ability of buyers to force down prices or bargain for higher quality/services

5. Which of the following factors makes suppliers powerful?
a. A diverse group of suppliers
b. Availability of substitutes
c. Unimportance of product/service to industry
d. Low switching costs for buyers

Answer: b. Availability of substitutes (if substitutes are not readily available, suppliers can be powerful)

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Here are some additional MCQs based on the information provided in this part:

1. Which of the following is a possible entry barrier based on access to distribution channels?
a. Economies of scale
b. Product differentiation
c. Capital requirements
d. Lack of shelf space in supermarkets

Answer: d. Lack of shelf space in supermarkets

2. What is the bargaining power of suppliers?
a. The ability of suppliers to raise prices or reduce quality
b. The ability of buyers to force down prices or bargain for higher quality/services
c. The threat of new entrants bringing new capacity and resources
d. The presence of substitute products or services

Answer: a. The ability of suppliers to raise prices or reduce quality

3. Which of the following factors makes buyers powerful?
a. The ability to forward integrate
b. Uniqueness of the product or service
c. Unimportance of the product or service to the industry
d. Low cost to change suppliers

Answer: d. Low cost to change suppliers

4. Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of a substitute product?
a. It satisfies the same need as another product
b. It has a different appearance
c. It can perform the same function as another product
d. It is a unique product or service

Answer: d. It is a unique product or service

5. Which of the following barriers to entry is based on the need to invest huge financial resources?
a. Economies of scale
b. Capital requirements
c. Switching costs
d. Product differentiation

Answer: b. Capital requirements
